sourceMovie.title: Pom Poko
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Pom Poko for the same reason it broke through in 1994: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a animation rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Isao Takahata keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The adventure and animation blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Princess Mononoke
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Pom Poko clicked for you, Princess Mononoke (1997) is a strong next stop: both trade in adventure and animation, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Pom Poko clicked for you, Epic (2013) is a strong next stop: both trade in adventure and animation, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Pom Poko?
faq.answer: Try Princess Mononoke, Epic, Ainbo: Spirit of the Amazon—each shares DNA with Pom Poko while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Pom Poko?
faq.answer: Pom Poko (1994) is best described as Adventure, Animation, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Pom Poko worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this adventure lane.
